frameworks.name: mechanism design
frameworks.one_line: designing rules of interaction without rationality, baseline, or fixed-objective assumptions

frameworks.name: learning-from-human-preferences
frameworks.one_line: mechanism parameters are tuned via direct participant preference feedback

frameworks.name: self-play electoral contest
frameworks.one_line: candidate mechanisms compete against copies of themselves to elicit preferences

frameworks.name: resource allocation game
frameworks.one_line: stylized testbed pitting productivity, equality, and free-riding against one another

core_question: Can a general-purpose method learn value-aligned mechanisms directly from human preferences without strong prior assumptions?
key_assumptions: participants can express preferences over mechanism variants
mechanism quality can be improved through iterative electoral competition
no a priori rationality, baseline, or welfare objective is required
manipulations: mechanism parameters adjusted via self-play preference contests
comparison against a strong baseline mechanism
deployment on a resource allocation game with productivity-equality tension
key_findings: HCMD-zero produces a mechanism humans prefer over a strong baseline
method works without prior knowledge or hand-specified objectives
human behavioral trajectories are used sparingly and effectively
interpretations: value-aligned mechanisms can be learned end-to-end from preference data
self-play electoral contests provide a scalable signal for mechanism improvement
